title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four amateur photographers who completed a documentary project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Julian, The Vanishing Trades
'I wanted to document the last traditional tailors in the city,' Julian says. [51]The hardest part was gaining the trust of the subjects, who were suspicious of someone with a camera intruding on their workspace.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my observational skills in my daily life became much more acute, which helped me in my hobby of birdwatching.[/48] [53]I learned that a good photograph is 10% equipment and 90% the relationship you build with the person in front of the lens.[/53] [50]I realized that my background as a history teacher helped me provide a rich context for the images in my final exhibition.[/50] [47]Initially, the cramped and dusty conditions of the workshops were a shock to my system.[/47] It was a deeply moving project.
texts.B:
### Sarah, The Night Shift
Sarah photographed people working while the city slept. 'It was a world of shadows,' she explains. [47]I hadn't expected the mental fatigue of reversing my sleep cycle to affect my creativity so much in the first month.[/47] [54]What kept me going was the quiet camaraderie I found with the night workers, who were happy to share their stories.[/54] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep sense of empathy I’d feel for the people who keep the city running invisibly.[/52] [49]The experience taught me that our perception of a 'normal' day is incredibly narrow and privileged.[/49] [56]If I hadn't won a small arts council grant, I never could have afforded the high-quality film I needed.[/56] It was a profound shift in my worldview.
texts.C:
### Victor, The Urban Decay Project
Victor documented abandoned industrial buildings. 'It’s about finding beauty in the broken,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the physical dangers of the sites, which required a level of caution and planning I hadn't expected.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general patience and ability to stay still for long periods improved significantly, which was a great mental side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that these ruins are not just empty shells, but monuments to a way of life that has passed.[/53] [50]I found that the focus I used in my previous career as a safety inspector was perfect for assessing the risks of entering the buildings.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the heavy equipment and the tripod, which felt quite cumbersome at first.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried the subject matter would be too bleak, but the light was often stunning.
texts.D:
### Elena, The Refugee Kitchen
'I wanted to show the power of food to bring people together,' Elena says. [49]The project made me realize that my own problems are insignificant compared to the resilience of those who have lost everything.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the project if my local community center hadn't invited me to document their integration program.[/56] [54]I was moved by the generosity of the families, who insisted on sharing their meals with me even when they had very little.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the emotional weight of the stories I heard to be so overwhelming and draining initially.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of editing hundreds of photos down to just ten incredibly stressful and difficult.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I wouldn't be able to do justice to their stories, but the families loved the final results.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or psychological impact of the project's environment or schedule?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) mentions the shock of cramped/dusty workshops, Sarah (B) the toll of the night shift, and Elena (D) the emotional weight of stories.

questions.question: notes a positive cognitive or personal improvement that transferred to another interest?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) saw his observational skills help with birdwatching, and Victor (C) improved his patience.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perception regarding societal privilege or personal resilience?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) reflects on the privilege of a 'normal' day, and Elena (D) on the resilience of refugees.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous to the project?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) used his history teacher background, and Victor (C) used his safety inspector skills.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to gaining access or ensuring personal safety?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) struggled to gain the trust of subjects, and Victor (C) with the physical dangers of the sites.

questions.question: admits to having had prior concerns or having formed a deep emotional connection through the work?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt deep empathy for workers, and Elena (D) worried she wouldn't do justice to the stories.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about the nature of the craft or the subject?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) learned a photo is about the relationship, and Victor (C) saw ruins as monuments.

questions.question: highlights the value of the generosity or camaraderie of the people they met?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) found camaraderie with night workers, and Elena (D) was moved by the generosity of the families.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the technical or administrative aspects?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to the heavy equipment, and Elena (D) found the editing process stressful.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external factor or invitation that led to the project's start?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) mentions an arts council grant, and Elena (D) was invited by a community center.
